item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's a picture of the Atlantic trade routes in my 1903 Andrees Allgemeiner Handatlas. I've released it under Creative Commons, and you are absolutely free to use it on the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to ask, why don't they just have a researcher slam their fist onto the page? Then they would be able to reset it pretty easily, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really good. Would read more of this.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried, but I can't restrain myself;

"Simpsons did it!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, the foundation is being truly menaced by micropeople! This is absolutely incredible. Upvoted with all my heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I have the luck to be in the possession of an actual Andrees Allgemeiner Handatlas from 1903, I have a few questions/thoughts:

-One of the very first maps in the real atlas is a map of the Solar System. What would happen if you left SCP-1235 open there?
-Likewise, what would happen if you opened the skip to pages where there are multiple copies of the world map, such as the pages showing ethnic and religion data?

Finally, as a bit of interesting trivia, the real 1903 Atlas's Atlantic Ocean map is on pages 20-21. Page 7-8 seem to be meteorological maps of the world IIRC. Then again, that's what makes SCP-1235 so anomalous, doesn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs like this are utterly fascinating to me. I'd love to see a tale about the aftermath of their attempted escape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who finds this oddly reminiscent of a Victoria II game on crack?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a book I read as a kid, The Boy Who Reversed Himself. The titular boy is the newest member of an organization dedicated to protecting the secret of moving in n-dimensional space, and protecting those who live in lower dimensional spaces. Essentially, every n-space world has an (n-1)-space bubble/portal thing inside of it that is its own separate world (down to the 1D world and up to some vaguely defined upper limit), and every world's organization needs to protect those that live in their bubble (and thus those in the bubble's bubble and so on), or risk their world being destroyed by the higher levels as vengeance. Beings can reach into the bubble and perform 'acts of divinity' as you mentioned, and being who learn how to move along the (n+1) axis can exit the bubble, though they are still 'flat' on the (n+1) axis and are also tiny in comparison to regular beings of (n+1) dimensional space. The characters had to wear special glasses that were capable of distilling the information of the 4D world into 3D visuals to interact with their 4D counterparts. The background conflict of the book was that the house in 3D space that holds the 2D world in its basement is under threat of being seized under eminent domain laws and being destroyed (along with the 2D world and then our world by proxy) in order to build a new highway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes, any molecules of poison gas that we could generate would be much too large to affect the hundreds of millions of inhabitants of this micro-world. They're not breathing the oxygen in the office, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it, would macroscopic poison gas be too big to hit them? cause you could poison them all and close the book while theyre dead, then it would reset.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, this is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this, absolutely love it.

One quibble, does the Foundation not have a huge amount of weapons/technology/SCPs which are all capable of combatting the micro-drones?
Also, is the containment breach an ongoing thing? like, we still haven't managed to close the map?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is delightful.

I'm surprised you didn't go for the obvious and attach a tilt-shift photograph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, with all my heart, this.

item.title: Re: Faux latin
item.content:encoded: UNPEEVED (hopefully). The naked eye view is indeed metadata that updates (cf. the picture caption) and which disappears at a certain level of magnification via some refractive trickery, though I didn't want to explore that too much as I felt it detracted from the hook. I originally had it that it only appeared photorealistic through 1235-1, but that created a whole lot of other problems and I dropped it.

item.title: Faux latin
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a time-compressed microworld is really cool, but if we are supposedly looking through the telescope at a real microworld, shouldn't the maps be photorealistic too? Is the naked-eye view is some kind of metadata, that changes as microcosmos boundaries do, or is it just camoflage? You address this a bit in the lead of the article, but I think it could use some fleshing out.

Also, neither "viri" or "virii" (both of which appear) is correct. The plural of virus is "viruses". PEEVE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a problem only Ant-Man can solve!

(In reality: Ant-Man is promptly eaten.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what sort of damage a microscopic 682 could wreak if it got loose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the most horrifying implication. I can completely believe it would happen.

It's like when kids on the playground did that thing where they go, "Are you afraid of a man this small?" and then clap their hands together and accuse you of blinking. Except the man is even smaller, and you should be very afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The implications just keep coming.

-2 has a microscopically perforated baffle well beyond the cpabilities of the time to make. I wonder how the original owner managed to get a whole bunch of really tiny holes precisely punched?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fascinating. I know an article deserves an upvote when I get to the end and wish there were more written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: but would burning destroy it? Or would we have thousands of micro-space colonies made of ash fragments?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sad thing is, if it ever does get set to keter they'd just have to burn it all, wouldn't they?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I had a bit of a mapgasm when I read the image caption, then became disappointed when I found out this wasn't just an anomalous map with different African colonies.

By the time I got down to the itty Foundation with its itty XK-class Keter containment, I had to upvote though. I love the continued interplay between micro and macro, and the fact that they can perceive our world and affect it. I have to wonder how the folks on the other pages feel about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like a cross between 881 and the inch-long wormhole that keeps tossing miniature nukes into our timespace. I think it actually combines the two ideas, along with the "they're in an atlas" angle, to make something that stands well enough on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine the old religions of the giants in the sky that seem to stay there for an entire age. Each researcher representing different ages of man. Since everything is sped up, right? Every 20-60 microyears a new researcher sticks his nose through 1235-1, become the god of the age, until the first closing of the book, then they decide they are evil and yadayada.

Have a cult that continually sends messages in a bottle outside of the map. Details on the current state of the map/world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Put that way, it reminds me a lot of SCP-1733, except one where they can break out of the loop. That, and they have physical entities they can blame, rightly or wrongly, for their condition. Which could throw a third possibility in the religion debate… it's possible there's a faction that worships the macroverse.

Hmm… that does add interest to the philosophical conundrums of the loop… Is it possible there's a faction somewhere in the SCP that supports the idea of allowing the loops to happen, and thus is quietly in opposition to the whole idea of clamping the pages open? If the whole idea of loops as the afterlife is accepted by some, do they see this whole operation as messing with the natural order of things, or even the equivalent of a mission storming heaven to burn it down?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All fantastic ideas - the whole ethical issue of whether it is moral to reset such a world (especially if inhabitants remember previous cycles) is something I considered getting into while drafting this but realised it would probably double the size of the article. The fact that people born in the microcosm after 01/03/1903 wouldn't appear during the next cycle is one I hadn't considered but which gives a certain poignancy to the fact that the microcosm is taking such extreme measures to prevent another reset. As Drewbear points out the continuity of memory over the resets also help to explain 1235's rapid technological advancement without recourse to micro-SCPs.

I have considered penning a Tale in the form of an SCP entry from the perspective of the microverse's 1903 Foundation exploring this scenario further (with or without the hemispheric extinction event) - obviously from their perspective they are 'our' Foundation (having all the memories leading up to 01/03/1903), suddenly forced to deal with a permanent breach of secrecy after the appearance of a 'macrocosmos'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another thought: this time around they can catch criminals before they commit their crimes. But is it legal to incarcerate or punish someone for a crime that literally didn't happen this time around? Is murder still a crime when the victim will be alive again the next reset? What about criminals that were already in prison with a life sentence? A life sentence has a very different meaning when your subjective lifespan (as measured by the continuance of your mind) is now theoretically infinite.

What happens to infants or very yougn children? In 20 years they grow into youths in the prime of their lives, only to become infants again, with unformed brains. Do they retain all the knowledge from the previous cycle? What does that do, shoving the mind of an adult into an incompletely formed brain? Or do they become a sort of special class of people, simultaneously envied and pitied for being new again each time around? Or do they become irreconcillably insane due to their infant brains essentially "blowing up" from the adult mind? And how do the parents cope with it? Will the children eventually all end up in state-sponsored orphanages as their parents become unable/unwilling to watch their children become strangers over and over again?

What happens to religion? Does atheism spike as people reject the idea of the existence of God and Heaven, since they know that they just reset? Does religious belief actually go up instead, as people believe this to be a trial God has set before them, like in the Old Testament? Do people go to their graves calm and secure in the knowledge that they'll wake again, or do they go screaming because the afterlife is denied them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Original and engaging.

I wonder if Lord Blackwood has met these guys?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
O_O

I had not thought thru the implications of knowledge retention at all. Groundhogs Day, but the entire planet gets to be Bill Murray. I like this even more than I thought I did.

I don't know that I buy 1 year, there is a lot of work to do even if you alrady have the knowledge. But twenty years is certainly feasible, even ten.

This is edging past "something I want to see tales about" into "something I want to see novels about."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I prefer the "slow-time" version, as a horror from the perspective of the microcosm. Imagine the first few ships out: from the perspective of ground control, the ships just slow to a stop a certain distance above the map. The sense of despair as they think that they're trapped and doomed with nothing that can save them… until they realize that the ships are just operating incredibly slowly. And the reactions from the first crew: they turn around when they stop receiving transmissions from ground control, only to find that months have passed in the few minutes (from their perspective) that they were gone.

It's like the time dilation effect of near-luminal travel, only on an observable scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really excellent, engaging article. Only thing that I noticed (and this is minor!) is that 3 weeks of being open would mean the microcosmic date is no later than 4/25/1920. Is their history so divergent from ours that they developed space (or possibly dimensional) travel that early? Or is that a result of their cumulative knowledge from all previous resets?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's also the fact that while the internal timeline gets reset to 1901, the inhabitants are still aware of what happened. If I'm reading this correctly, it would be like if everyone in the real world suddenly reset to exactly where they were in, say, 01/01/2001 at 1430 GMT. We'd be back in our bodies, in the exact same location and circumstance that we were then, but we'd all remember the time we'd lived up until August 6, 2012. So now the world would play out differently, because we all have 12 more years of memories. And it keeps happening at random intervals. Even if the physical world resets, we still have all the knowledge, so we don't have to start our scientific development over from scratch, just the infrastructure.

So this time, we know when all the various natural disasters are going to be and fully evacuate before hand. The birth rate probably plummets as people find that they just can't cope with repeatedly having kids, only for them to disappear forever during the next reset (because the odds of the exact same sperm meeting the exact same egg again are effectively zero). People who were in the process of dying right at the reset (caught in a house fire, for instance) are trapped in an unending (from their perspective) hell of pain and death. And this time, we know where all the natural resources that we need to escape are. So people are READY to immediately start building spaceships the instant that the reset happens. Given the massive incentive to do so, we'd probably have a warfleet built and launched within a year (roughly a day macroscale)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers :) I've taken another pass at it, removing technical language not vital to the hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eek, that refers back to an earlier draft where the map was published in 1903, but depicted the world in 1901; I simplified this as it was needlessly complex but apparently failed to change the reset date :(

I did have a section where I pontificated about how the central European powers' rapid buildup of advanced technology might be the result of overt aid being provided by the Foundation and other Groups of Interest (the appearance of the macroverse constituting the end of the masquerade). I got rid of it as I felt it muddied the waters too much, and had already removed a grimdark bit where the hemisphere not depicted had 'apparently undergone an extinction event' (due to lack of light) as tonally wrong for the article and impossible for the macro-Foundation to know anyway. I think we can assume that the microcosmos is using everything at their disposal to ensure their survival, which would include any anomalous items already found and catalogued at the time :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, you say time starts at date of publication, 1903; but next line says that closing and re-opening resets everything to 1901. I guess that could be an intentional disjoint, but it reads like a typo.

Liliputians to the nth degree? I like it. I would love to see the reverse of this, the contianment procedures Foundation-Mini is attempting to enact against Foundation-Macro.

Dust from our scale must be like a constant barrage of massive meteors pummeling their world. No wonder they are hostile.

A 1:300 time ratio is a bit less than one year of map-time per day. So they went from the Wright brothers to the equivalent of a fleet of interplanetary battleships in less than 20 years?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted just because this is so damn weird. My. Only complaint would be that the paragraph describing 1235-1 (and to a lesser extent the next paragraph) are so laden with science-babble as to be obtuse and difficult to slog through. I might recommend simplifying it a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pardon a small complaint, but you give the publication date as 1903… yet "adlerauge MCMVII" means "eagle eye 1907".

Otherwise, fascinating.

(German word for "published": veröffentlicht.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderfully done. Excellent science. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Likewise. I would also be keen to see some sort of experimental log, detailing the autopsy of one of the microcosm's inhabitants or perhaps an attempt to reverse engineer their technology onto a macro-scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit too much science jargon for my taste, which pulled me out of it some, since I had to look up a few words, but the idea and general execution I like alot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahaha. Fucking brilliant. I'd love to see a tale from the perspective of these guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks :) Gone through and diversified the language where possible - in some cases I've gone for 'SCP-1351' or 'the inhabitants of SCP-1351'. I didn't really need to specify that what exploded in Professor Blank's ear was microcosmic high-explosive as the mass would be the same, and I also hopefully didn't need to explain that what's filling habitat spheres for people 600 angstroms tall is microcosmic air - cut both references.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I noticed that too. You seem to be rather in love with the term "microcosm".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You use microcosmic waaaay too many times in the last addendum but otherwise this is stellar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I once had an idea that was sort of like this, only the "world" was completely 2-dimensional, and its inhabitants were incapable of percieving the 3-dimensional "real" world; consequently, any interaction by Foundation staff was perceived as acts of divinity by the inhabitants of the 2D world.

Oh yeah, and it was recovered from an MC&D auction.

This is pretty good, though. Now I'm worried my idea might clash a bit with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another great example of how the Foundation doesn't really know what it's doing and how easily something seemingly innocuous can spiral Pulitzer of control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
containment breach of a microcosmic version of SCP-███ constitutes a possible XK-class event and it is not at all clear that this would be confined to the microcosmos.

oh shiiiiiiiiiiiiiii-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, not sure on that one. My first response was 'yes' - the accelerated rate of time applies to all matter originating from 1235. That would mean that Incident 1235-01 would have taken months from the perspective of the microcosmonauts, and helps to explain how they're able to move macroscopic objects. However, it would also be very cool to have the microcosmonauts returning from macro-space to find that years had passed on 'Earth'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, and the execution's great, but I did have one question. While the microcomic ships and their crew operate in macrocosmic space, is the accelerated rate of time still active?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good. This is the type of article I like.

Side-note: My headcannon now is that 699 is the equivalent of the microdot things they dropped onto the atlas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Old-style Darwinia, not bad.
Though ,makes me wonder how does the external world, and the apparent knowledge of it by the microcosm's denizens affect their society. (Example would be the attack on the researcher, or capture and modification of macro-world viri)
This has quite some tale potential, if you can pull it off believably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*stares*

*stares further*

*claps slowly, upvotes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated as suggested :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my first draft it was more specifically linked as it was specially designed to view microcosmic light. I removed it as I didn't want to introduce microcosm physics before I'd actually established that's what SCP-1235 was. Would reinstating that help the connection?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't actually help: now they're just unrelated objects with a common source.
